data.title: Chimera Investment Corporation Notes Analysis: CIMP Gets The Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

A previous article analyzed the four preferred stocks issued by Chimera Investment Corporation (CIM). Here, I move up the capital structure to analyze the three Notes available to investors, those being:

  • Chimera Investment Corp 9.000% Senior Notes due 5/15/2029, Chimera Investment Corporation CAL 29 (CIMN).
  • Chimera Investment Corp 9.250% Senior Notes due 08/15/2029, Chimera Investment Corporation NT 29 (CIMO).
  • Chimera Investment Corp 8.875% Senior Notes due 8/15/2030, Chimera Investment Corporation NT 30 (CIMP).

Based on its better YTC, YTM, last Maturity date, longest built-in call protection, and least likely to get Called, my choice, thus the Buy rating, goes to CIMP.

Chimera Investment Corporation Review

The best due diligence starting point for Notes is with the issuer, as one needs to gauge if there is interest and/or redemption risk in owning these assets.

Seeking Alpha describes this mREIT as (edited):

Chimera Investment Corporation operates as a real estate investment trust in the United States. The company, through its subsidiaries, invests in a portfolio of mortgage assets, including residential mortgage loans, non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities, agency mortgage-backed securities secured, agency mortgage-backed securities secured by pools of commercial mortgage loans, business purpose and investor loans, and other real estate-related securities.

Source: Seeking Alpha CIM.

Chimera describes themselves as:

We are a credit-focused hybrid mortgage REIT, meaning that our portfolio contains residential mortgage loans and both Agency and non-Agency mortgage-backed securities. Our hybrid approach allows us to maintain flexibility in portfolio allocation and liability management. Through the use of securitization and leverage, we aim to create long-term value for our shareholders.

We invest in RMBS issued by third parties or issued or guaranteed by Ginnie Mae, Fannie Mae, or Freddie Mac (Agency RMBS). Our Agency CMBS assets are primarily Ginnie Mae Construction Loan and Ginnie Mae Permanent Loan Certificates with prepayment protection.

Source: Chimera REIT.

The latest investor presentation highlighted the following, starting with their approach to the mortgage market.

The next diagram illustrates their strategy for building long-term shareholder value.

The next two tables provide details on their holdings portfolio.

The last line above shows the delinquency rate; the next shows how those rates have been changing since late 2022.

Now I will turn to the financial statements to get a gauge on the safety of the interest payments and the par value of the Notes. First is the income statement, where I compare net income to Notes interest owed.

With my calculations showing CIM owes just over $22MM in interest on their Notes, coverage is just over 10X, a solid coverage ratio.

Next, I look at the balance sheet to see how much equity is available to pay off the par value of the Notes, which I calculated at $245MM.

Again, the ratio is just over 10X; thus, I would rate defaulting as very low.

Comparing the Notes

Too bad I didn’t submit this article at the start of April, as Note prices are up sharply since then. Looking for a reason, I found an article about Tudor Investment Corporation raising its share of the outstanding common by 35% to about a 0.4% stake. The article also noted there has been insider buying recently.

The following table compares the key features across the three Notes and the Chimera Investment Corp. 8.00% Series B Fixed/Float Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Stock, Chimera Investment Corporation PFD SER B (CIM.PR.B), which earned a Buy rating in my last article for comparing Notes to the best Preferred stock.

Factor CIMN CIMO CIMP PFD D
Issued 5/15/24 8/12/24 9/9/25 2/22/17
Size (MM shares) 2.6 2.6 4.6 12.0
Coupon 9.00% 9.25% 8.875% 9.75%*
Price $25.50 $25.68 $25.44 $24.14
Yield 8.8% 9.1% 8.9% 10.2%
Call date 5/15/26 8/15/26 8/15/27 3/30/24
YTC NA** .8% 7.5% NA
Maturity date 5/15/29 8/15/29 8/15/30 NA
YTM 8.3% 8.3% 8.4% NA

* Floating formula: 3-month SOFR + 6.052%.

** I chose not to provide YTC as my calculator isn’t precise enough for such a short time remaining.

Both CIMO and CIMP were rated as BBB by Egan-Jones Ratings when issued, which seems to be the only rating they ever assign. I did not see in any Prospectus that Chimera was allowed to delay interest payments as some Notes allow. With the 5-year UST rate being very volatile since CIMN was issued, it is hard to tell if the markets changed their demand for what it costs CIM to issue each Note.

Analysis

  • All Note coupons are fixed, whereas the preferred has a very attractive floating formula.
  • As investors might expect, PFD-D has a better yield, given that it falls below the Notes in the capital structure.
  • Even accounting for its pending interest payment, CIMN would have a negative YTC.
  • CIMO, at current prices, has a slightly higher yield.
  • CIMP has the best YTC and YTM values, and its lowest coupon should make it the last Note Called once all three are eligible.

Conclusion

Based on its better YTC, YTM, last Maturity date, longest built-in Call protection, plus the least likely to get Called, my choice, thus the Buy rating, goes to CIMP. That said, the yield on the preferred makes it an attractive choice.

Portfolio strategy

If an investor wants to bypass funds for preferred stocks and select and weigh issues individually, here are the factors that I think should come into their due diligence process.

  • Coupon type: Fixed rates provide exact knowledge of what the dividend will be, whereas floating rates will move when market rates move.
  • Payment risk: As covered here, is the yield provided at purchase enough to compensate for the potential risk of non-payment.
  • Floating formula: The higher the fixed add-on rate, the better (mostly). I like ones using the 5-year UST rate versus the 3-month SOFR, though the shorter SOFR has benefits too. One caveat: a generous floating formula increases the chance of being Called.
  • Call date: A common feature is five years of Call protection. That said, many have covenants that allow for early Calls.
  • Maturity date: Unlike most preferred stocks, almost all Notes have a maturity date. The longer that is, the greater the odds the coupon floats.
  • Selection: By this I’m referring to, as is the case with Chimera, which coupon is best to own? By not owning the highest coupon, the investor should gain extra Call protection.

The interplay of these factors and where interest rates are in the market will help determine what the yield, YTC, and any YTM investors can collect by buying the issue.

data.title: JinkoSolar: Post-Earnings Weakness, Same Old Risks
data.description:
Thesis

This would be my third time talking about JinkoSolar Holding Co. (JKS), which is one of the biggest solar companies in the world. The company makes all the main parts needed for solar power, starting from wafers and cells all the way to finished solar panels, and sells them to customers around the world, including large utility projects, businesses, and homes.

Looking at my past notes, JinkoSolar is one of those companies that sat on the precipice of a “Sell” call for me. But one of the reasons it tilted just enough toward a “Hold” was mainly because its scale and TOPCon technology suggested real long-term potential. Still, between thin profits, climbing debt, and solar prices that won’t stop sliding, there wasn’t much reason to turn bullish either.

Since that call, the stock’s taking a beating, and yesterday’s post-earnings reaction didn’t do them any favors either. Now that I’ve had a chance to catch up and review the latest data and fundamental factors, I’m maintaining my neutral stance because the turnaround case is still too fragile to justify getting bullish, while the downside is just not severe enough to warrant a sell.

JinkoSolar Q4 2025: The Cost of a Buyer’s Market

  • Q4 shipments: 26 GW
  • FY2025 shipments: 86 GW
  • Shipment YoY: -7.3%
  • Q4 revenue: $2.5B
  • Q4 revenue YoY: -15%

The core problem is familiar to anyone watching Chinese solar manufacturers right now. There's an extreme amount of excess manufacturing capacity as well as restricted demand, and at the same time the buyers for this product have been trained to be patient. When they know that, over the longer term, prices will continue downward, they'll hold off on making any purchases. This dynamic has resulted in the procurement side of the market being trained to wait for better quotes; this leaves manufacturers in a defensive position no matter the number of gigawatts shipped. This was exactly how Jinko's fiscal quarter unfolded. They shipped a significant amount of modules, but the prices were too soft for this shipping volume to create any significant earnings.

  • Q4 gross margin: 0.3%
  • Q3 gross margin: 7.3%
  • Silver price increase: +250–300%

As a result, the margin shrank despite shipments growing much larger. Jinko cited rising costs for silver, some pressure from polysilicon, and unfavorable foreign exchange as reasons for margin compression, but silver is clearly the largest contributor to this margin compression. As a result, manufacturers have been forced to investigate replacing silver with copper wherever possible. With costs and customer pricing out of sync, buyers stayed opportunistic and largely refused to absorb higher module prices.

  • Module ASP range: ~$0.115–$0.14/W
  • High-efficiency shipments (Q4): ~3 GW
  • TOPCon lab efficiency: 27.79%

JinkoSolar plans to use its product line (the mix) as a marketing strategy which consists primarily of promoting its higher-efficiency TOPCon modules. Jinko believes that the improved performance of these modules will maintain higher average selling prices through the first half of the year. There's merit to this argument because customers who are concerned about land-use efficiency, energy yield, and overall system economics value the performance of solar panels. Therefore, in principle, they'll be willing to pay a premium for higher-performing products. However, this premium shouldn't be assumed. That advantage holds up best when supply is tight and when solar projects are making good returns. It just isn't a guarantee.

  • Q4 operating expenses: $473.6M
  • OpEx QoQ: +28%
  • Accounts receivable days: 94
  • Inventory days: 75
  • Q4 operating cash flow: $470M

Looking at the operating expense pool, a marked rise in the quarter can be chiefly attributed to impairments rather than underlying business deterioration. This makes analyzing operating leverage much more difficult. However, removing this distortion shows a more reassuring pattern in working capital. For example, receivable collections are moving faster, inventories are being reduced more quickly, and operating cash flow is significantly stronger. My point is, these factors carry weight in light of the solar industry downturn; all of the above point towards a company that maintains some measure of control over the business, albeit alongside an uncomfortable income statement. How a company handles cash collection and inventory usually shows whether it’s steering through cycles or just going along with them. On those fronts, JinkoSolar appears to have its act together.

  • Cash & equivalents: $3.3B
  • Total debt: $6.7B
  • Net debt: $3.44B
  • FY2025 operating cash flow: $280M
  • CapEx 2025: ~$1B
  • CapEx 2026: ~$700M

Liquidity and balance-sheet strength, however, are not synonymous. Cash balances for JinkoSolar were largely unchanged quarter-on-quarter; even so, both total and net debt were still high. This, in turn, limits the company’s flexibility if the contraction in demand for solar products continues for longer than expected. While generating positive operating cash flow for an entire quarter is clearly a step in the right direction, it doesn't really resolve the longer-term structural pressures from low margins, high capital intensity, and high financing costs. Given all that, the plan to dial back capital expenditures doesn’t go unnoticed. The majority of recent investments have been in upgrading technology rather than expanding capacity, and I'd say that this is probably the most logical decision given that there's too much production capacity in the market. Now we just need to see if these investments lead to improved margins, or simply prolong JinkoSolar’s ability to endure adverse conditions. On this front, JinkoSolar is still asking for the benefit of the doubt.

  • ESS shipments FY2025: 5.2 GW
  • ESS revenue recognized: 1.7 GWh
  • ESS orders backlog: >10 GWh
  • ESS margin target: 10–15%

For me, JinkoSolar's energy storage segment is an increasingly interesting aspect of its business. The rationale for this move is fairly straightforward; the emphasis in both growing and mature (developed) markets, especially the United States, is shifting from the lowest unit cost of a solar module to attributes that enhance dispatchability, supply-chain compliance, and overall system performance. This change is being nudged along by new government rules. In particular, the introduction FEOC regulations is making the requirements stricter for companies to qualify for tax credits. The effect is more focus on vetted supply chains and keeping solar and storage manufacturing at home. Accordingly, storage, as a path to move beyond the commodity nature of solar modules, appears to be a sensible way to achieve this objective by improving economics and increasing the durability of customer relationships.

  • Overseas shipment mix: ~60%
  • U.S. shipment mix: ~5%
  • U.S. shipment target 2026: 5–10%
  • Market share 2025: ~13–14%

Geography is now carrying about as much weight as product mix in business decisions. JinkoSolar is investing more budget dollars into selling products outside of China, with higher-efficiency products that have a brand name, along with stronger bankable credit ratings than traditional Chinese products. The urge to go international makes sense, as far as it goes. But the downside to an expanded international market presence are potentially more complicated regulations, providing a broader range of litigation, and a much greater sensitivity to changes in international trade policy. We can pivot back to the United States as an example, where recent pricing increases have come from stricter enforcement and compliance with the requirements of the FEOC. These regulatory standards may provide manufacturers the ability to maintain steady profit margins, but they increase the risk associated with fulfilling customer orders, establishing new customers, and the need to source product from a local country.

JKS: The Illusion of Value

From the right angle, JKS can be made to look cheap. Its EV/Sales ratio is about 0.58x based on trailing results and 0.42x based on expected results. By comparison, the average for other companies in the same sector is much higher, around 3.48x trailing and 3.11x forward.

Meanwhile, price to sales is 0.13x trailing. Price to book is 0.53x trailing. Compared to its own past, those forward valuation multiples for JKS are much lower than where the stock has usually traded. In my experience, when a stock sinks that far, it’s more often a warning than a missed opportunity.

The actual earnings don’t really contradict that cautious view. Right now, the numbers look pretty weak: the trailing EV to EBITDA ratio is 52.83×, which is very high, while profitability is very low, with a gross margin of just 3.13% and an EBITDA margin of only 1.11%. EBIT margin is negative 9.69%. Net margin is negative 4.99%.

Revenue falling 34.27% year over year doesn't help. Now throw in an EBITDA that collapsed 93.64%.

Analysts do expect EPS to improve a lot for JinkoSolar.

Based on current estimates, the stock would trade at about 11.93× earnings in 2026 and 5.71× in 2027, which, again, looks quite cheap, but only if margins magically recover instead of limping along where they are.

There's definitely some good that can come from all of this, but it's also clearly only going to happen if certain criteria are met. The management at JinkoSolar has laid out a logical sequence of objectives: preserve cash flow, continue to shift the sales focus toward higher-value customers and products, increase the amount of storage in higher-margin markets, and rely more on tech enhancements to drive margin recovery. There’s some thought behind the strategy, at least. On top of that, JKS also looks cheap when you compare its share price to its revenue or book value. But that kind of cheapness doesn’t mean much on its own. What counts is turning sales and assets into profits, and that part is still unresolved.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I think that the appropriate stance, for now, is to continue to Hold. There's clear potential upside for JinkoSolar if its energy storage business grows, solar prices stop falling, and profit margins improve. But the risks are still very real: the core solar panel business isn’t very profitable, the company has a relatively high level of debt, and customers still have strong control over pricing.

data.title: Main Street Capital: The Moment Of Truth Has Arrived
data.description:
Main Street Capital Overview

Main Street Capital (MAIN) is, arguably, considered one of the best "sleep-well-at-night" BDCs in the entire sector.

MAIN has a powerful offering that it can bring to the table for durable income (and NAV) investors:

  1. The highest base dividend coverage level in the sector, conservative balance sheet, exposure to lower middle market segment (better risk-adjusted loan investment opportunities than in the core or upper segments), high-quality equity co-investment book, and so on (namely, robust fundamentals).
  2. Exceptional track record of raising (or maintaining) its monthly dividend for ~19 consecutive years since its IPO and delivering very strong total returns; just take a look at the chart below to see how MAIN has performed compared to the S&P 500 (SPY).

Because of these elements and the fact that MAIN is an internally managed BDC (no incentive and base management fees), it is only logical that MAIN trades at a premium to the sector.

However, MAIN's YTD performance has probably surprised its investors to the downside. MAIN is down ~12%, which is ~300 basis points below the sector index (BIZD). Similarly, just recently MAIN issued guidance for Q1 results, which instantly triggered a ~7% share price decline. Plus, from my recent analysis of the BDC short positions, it is evident that MAIN is among the most shorted players in the sector (i.e., ~7.5% short interest).

In the meantime, MAIN continues to trade at the highest P/NAV multiple (1.6x), which is meaningfully above the sector average of 0.76x.

So, if we put these points together, the logical question becomes whether we are at the early inning of a significant mean-reversion: meaning MAIN becoming valued closer to at least its internally managed peers (at around P/NAV 1.2x).

In my previous piece on MAIN, I commented that the BDC is in an overvalued bubble zone, which will most likely result in a negative alpha performance (underperformance to its peers). Since then, the stock has fallen by ~20%. However, I also conclude the piece on a relatively bullish note from the "durable and sustainable income investment perspective."

Let me now quickly unpack the current situation (including the data points on the Q1 earnings report).

Thesis review

Over the past ~4.5-month period, the gap between MAIN's P/NAV and that of Capital Southwest (CSWC), which is its closest peer, has significantly narrowed. Also, the distance from Trinity Capital (TRIN) multiple (an internally managed BDC) has decreased. The only exception, speaking of internally managed BDCs, is Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC), which has been very badly hit due to its notable exposure to the SaaS sector.

Having said that, MAIN's P/NAV continues to stand out as a huge extreme:

  • MAIN: P/NAV of 1.60x.
  • CSWC: P/NAV of 1.35x.
  • HTGC: P/NAV of 1.22x.
  • TRIN: P/NAV of 1.15x.

Now, let's pause here and explore what has changed (on a fundamental level) since MAIN released its Q4 2025 earnings figures (or since the moment when MAIN's share price started to tumble).

Three things.

First, the risks in the BDC system have increased mainly from these two items:

  1. Potentially damaged earnings prospects for SaaS names.
  2. Elevated redemptions from private BDCs.

However, for MAIN none of these items introduce a serious headache. The exposure to SaaS is well below the sector average of ~20% (i.e., below 10%). Plus, as a publicly traded BDC, MAIN does not have to worry about meeting investor liquidity requests.

Second, the interest rate outlook has changed. Earlier in the year, before the war in Iran broke out, the expectation was that we would experience several base rate cuts in 2026. Currently, the consensus is pricing in no cuts for 2026, which is positive for MAIN and BDCs in general (i.e., no headwinds from floating-rate loan investments). On top of that, the spreads have widened, which means that each dollar that MAIN puts to work is likely to generate higher NII than before.

Third, as mentioned above, preliminary results of Q1 have come out.

Here I would like to split the essence into two parts: the positive and the negative.

On the positive front, we have the following:

  • An estimated NAV of $33.42 to $33.50 per share, which means that MAIN is most likely to register a higher NAV per share relative to the prior quarter. It goes directly against the overall notion of BDCs falling apart from surging bankruptcies.
  • The LMM and traditional private credit loan books have expanded by $157.1M and $36.6M, respectively. MAIN's portfolio has become larger, which is good for the NII per share generation.

Now comes the negative part:

  • Non-accruals are up by 70 bps (4.0% in Q1 compared to 3.3% in the prior quarter). The fact that the non-accrual statistic is increasing is not good, but it is not to worry about either. For example, a 4.0% non-accrual level will still be lower than in Q1 2025 and Q2 2025 (4.5% and 5.0%, respectively). Plus, it will land very close in line with the sector average.
  • The distributable net investment income (DNII) is estimated to come in at $1.00 per share (I'm the middle point of the guided range). This is already more problematic, as it would imply that on a quarterly basis the DNII per share reduces by almost 9%.
  • As a result of the lower DNII and less pronounced NAV growth, the annualized ROE is projected to land at 6%, which is less than the current dividend yield of 7.5%.

The essence (or the key message) of the last point is that, based on Q1 value creation results (generated DNII and NAV growth), the current dividend is not fully supported.

In my view, there are two obvious drivers to this: 1) lower DNII due to the lagged effects from the previous base rate cuts, slightly rising non-accruals, and debt rollover at higher yields; 2) most likely no successful monetization of MAIN's equity stakes that would materially boost NAV (only logical given the Q1 turbulence).

With this in mind, let me now summarize and share my views on MAIN.

The bottom line

Even though MAIN's share price has meaningfully declined, the absolute and relative level of valuation remains aggressive (very rich). This opens up a logical question as to whether the P/NAV is really justified and whether MAIN has arrived close to the inflection point after which we won't see such a drastic valuation gap.

In my view, it is only logical that MAIN trades at a premium (both to NAV and its peers) because of the following reasons:

1) MAIN carries one of the most defensive fundamentals in the game: e.g., debt-to-equity of 0.82x (sector average - 1.25x), base dividend coverage level of 142% (sector average - 97%), and portfolio company interest coverage of 4.1x (sector average - 2.5x).

2) As of Q4 2025, MAIN had $7.10 per share of cumulative pre-tax net unrealized appreciation, which accounts for ~22% of the total NAV. When the market conditions for M&A activity improve, MAIN should be able to harvest at least part of this embedded value, which will directly stimulate NAV growth (and enable sizeable supplemental dividends as well as higher ROE).

3) Preliminary Q1 2026 figures indicate a continued stability (e.g., immaterial changes in non-accruals, NAV growth).

Yet I clearly disagree with the extent to which MAIN's multiple exceeds that of the closest peers. For example, I see no reason why MAIN should be priced ~18% above CSWC, which is a very similar peer with even lower exposure to SaaS, lower non-accruals, and similar exposure to high-quality LMM equity investments. The annualized 6% ROE is a first major sign that the underlying earnings don't justify such an extreme multiple.

So, as a result of this, I would suggest investors approach MAIN in the following way:

a) Keep the current stake in MAIN unchanged, as the base dividends and NAV will very likely remain stable (durable) going forward. Namely, MAIN continues to remain a fundamentally sound "sleep-well-at-night" income vehicle.

b) Divest MAIN and/or switch off DRIP if you want to avoid near-term turbulence (i.e., a further selloff driven by valuation convergence to its closest peers) and extract higher durable income streams from your BDC positions (think of other high-quality, lower-priced, internally managed BDCs such as CSWC and TRIN).

data.title: Voyager Technologies: NASA Mission Win Is Strategic, Not Financial
data.description:
Voyager Technologies (VOYG) has surged 8.8% following the win of a private astronaut mission from NASA. While this is a meaningful win for Voyager Technologies in terms of paving the way to their commercial space station called Starlab, we note that the stock price is only up 9.4% since my last report, in which I upgraded the stock from sell to buy. That is definitely better than the market, but a substantial part of the stock price increase comes from the stock price reaction after the mission award. That also makes it important to assess the value of the mission.

Voyager Technologies: On The Cross-Road Of Defense And Commercial Space

Voyager Technologies was established in 2019 and operates through three segments:

  1. Defense and National Security
  2. Space Solutions
  3. Starlab Space Solutions

In 2025, the company generated $166 million in sales, of which 74% was attributable to defense and national security and the remainder to the space business. We note that defense and national security were the growth engines supporting hypersonics, missile defense, kill vehicles, and re-entry vehicles. Space revenues, on the other hand, declined, driven by the completion of legacy NASA contracts.

The Starlab Space Solutions segment is an interesting one. The joint venture with Airbus (EADSF), MDA Space (MDA:CA), Mitsubishi Corporation, and Palantir (PLTR) aims to provide a replacement for the ISS for microgravity science and research in space. The ISS is set to be de-orbited around 2030, which could put the company at the forefront of facilitating research in space. We do note that Starlab is partially funded through NASA’s replacement program, and the current budget cuts at NASA could alter funding and replacement requirements.

We note that Voyager Technologies is aiming to grow organically and inorganically to become a vertically as well as horizontally integrated company.

The Significance Of The Private Astronaut Mission

NASA is trying to exit its role as the operator in low Earth orbit, or LEO missions. We see that clearly in the supply of the ISS where SpaceX and Boeing (BA) are contracted to fly the supply and recrewing missions, and in lunar exploration. For NASA, budget pressure is one element of why they are exiting LEO missions. At the same time, commercial solutions may be more efficient, and it allows NASA to shift focus to other areas, first to manned missions to the Moon and later on to deep-space missions. With the contract award to Voyager, the company is also breaking into a space that is dominated by Axiom, which has been awarded five missions. A sixth mission contract was awarded to Vast in February this year, and Voyager is now awarded the 7th mission contract.

For Voyager Technologies, I do not expect the contract to be a big-margin contract, and it may not be profitable at all. The contract award gives Voyager a seat in the program, and that was likely the first objective, and from there future awards may follow. So, this may be a loss leader contract. We also note that Voyager Technologies is not yet fully vertically or horizontally integrated, and they have no aspirations to provide launch services or launch vehicles. So, while it may have won the contract to be an operator on the mission, we note that substantial portions of the contract dollars will be required for launch services. Voyager’s non-pass-through revenues and profits will mostly be generated from mission operations.

For Voyager, I do not believe that the margins on this contract will be big, but it also does not need to have big margins. That may sound counterintuitive, but this contract award can be of importance when the company launches its own commercial space station called Starlab. The Private Astronaut Missions provide contractors with opportunities to build experience with human spaceflight and its economics before the ISS de-orbits. So, the PAM missions are about building experience and credibility, which should also pave the way for “creating demand.” In essence, no commercial crew is going to fly to an unproven station with an unproven mission operator. With PAM, that changes, and that is the bigger value for Voyager here besides breaking the Axiom dominance on the PAM missions. The PAMs are a stepping stone in the transition from ISS to commercial space stations, not a long-term mission program that is meant to last decades.

Voyager Technologies CapEx Grants Matter

For Voyager Technologies, we note that EBITDA margins are expected to decline even further this year before turning sharply higher in 2027. However, throughout the forecast period, the margins are not expected to turn positive, and the same holds for free cash flow. As capital expenditures increase, the free cash flow burn will improve but not be positive. The only reason why Voyager Technologies is not seeing a substantially worse free cash flow burn is because some of the capital expenditures are covered by grants. That also provides the main risk for Voyager Technologies. If grants do not come in the amounts that I am currently expecting, which is currently estimated at around $600 million through 2028, or there is a timing mismatch with capital expenditures, then we could see the cash position worsen significantly. So, that is something that investors need to keep in mind.

Since Voyager Technologies is not expected to generate positive EBITDA, I am using an EV/Sales method to value the company. What we see is that against the peer group multiple of 4.3x, there is no upside this year or next year, but against FY28 sales, there should be 48% upside. For emerging space companies, I do believe that a multiple equal to two times the aerospace and defense multiple can be applied. This would indicate a 15% downside for 2026. However, I do believe that the market generally is more forward-looking, and that would indicate a $42.94 price target, indicating 32% upside. We note that for 2028 there would be around 200% upside. However, I also do believe that as the company will be on the brink of generating positive EBITDA, we should at that point start considering different valuation methods.

Furthermore, we note that the company has a $460 million convertible loan outstanding with maturity in 2030. If we implement those on a pro-forma basis, we would see the 32%-48% upside slim to 10%-24%. I am taking the mid-point of that range as my base case price target, which would indicate a $38.07 price target, which is virtually unchanged from my prior $38.24 price target.

Conclusion: NASA Contract Is Important But Not Major Driver Of Upside

I believe that the NASA contract is an important one, as it gives the company a contracted opportunity to gain experience in human spaceflight and its economics. This will ultimately be an important consideration when Voyager and its joint-venture partners launch their own commercial space station. However, from a financial perspective, I believe that this is not a margin driver but more a learning curve that could, in the worst case, be loss-making. I am maintaining my speculative buy rating for the name, as I see no major reason to alter the rating in either direction due to the contract award.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TSMC Q1 Beats The Expectations, Guidance Raised As A Result Of AI-Driven Demand
data.description:
Introduction

Just a few days ago, I wrote an earnings preview about Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), and there I explained all of the reasons why this is the world’s best manufacturer, not only in the semiconductor industry but, most likely, overall. You can see it for yourself from the graph below, where I added some of the metrics, which show how well the company is doing.

The graph is rather messy with many metrics, but I believe it proves well how stable TSMC really is. The only extraordinary drop across the metrics was for gross margin during the pandemic, which most likely came from supply chain hurdles, and not something that was in TSMC's control.

Today, I want to break down the earnings the company has just announced, as the results were absolutely spectacular, beating across the board, and raising guidance. What is even more interesting is that the stock didn’t react as it could have, and it creates a great opportunity for starting the position right now, with the freshest information.

Q1 2026

In the earnings preview, I said that “their gross margins expanded this quarter to a staggering 62.3%, which is unheard of for a manufacturer,” but this quarter, the company has managed to surprise the market once again with 66.2%, guiding even higher than that in Q2. Just so we are clear, even at the level of the pandemic drop, the company could easily be included in the world's best manufacturers rating, as a 40% gross margin is incredible, since the company has to invest billions into manufacturing capacity before starting to sell, taking all the risks on themselves.

In general, manufacturing is known for being a tough industry, where companies often are forced to work with razor-thin margins, but TSMC doesn’t have this problem. An operating margin of 58.1% also came in ahead of the previous guidance, beating both the top and the bottom lines of the range between 54%-56%. Both operating and gross margins are seeing massive growth, not only YoY but also QoQ, and it means that the demand, especially for advanced computing for AI training and inference, is seeing organic growth.

In my latest article, I pointed out that the efforts of the TSMC to diversify its manufacturing away from Taiwan to minimize geopolitical risks with overseas manufacturing, while being a great idea, will come at the cost of dragging down margins in the next few years. This quarter, management pointed out that the guidance of margin expansion by 30 basis points accounts for the overseas buildout margin compression, so at least for now, the company offsets those risks brilliantly:

We have just guided our second quarter gross margin to increase by 30 basis points to 66.5% at the midpoint, primarily driven by a higher overall utilization rate and continued cost improvement efforts, including productivity gains, partially offset by dilution from our overseas fab.

But the margins are still expected to shrink not only due to the fabs costs in the U.S., Japan, and Germany, but also as a aftermath of 2nm chip production, which naturally has a lower gross margin, due to high expenses for the new technology research and development, but it should be temporary:

As we have said before, the initial ramp-up of our 2-nanometer technology will start to dilute our gross margin in the second half of this year, and we expect between 2% and 3% dilution for the full year of 2026. Furthermore, as the scale of our overseas expansion grows, we continue to forecast the gross margin dilution from the ramp-up of overseas fabs in the next several years to be 2% to 3% in the early stages and widen to 3% to 4% in the latter stages.

Finally, talking about the margins, I was pleasantly surprised to see management addressing the Middle East supply chain risks, which I mentioned in my previous article:

An interesting layer here is the war in the Middle East, though it might show up more in their future guidance than in this quarter. The conflict has made essentials like energy and raw materials more expensive and complicated to get, and it may impact the number of TSM as well, since Taiwan imports almost all of its energy. There's also a helium shortage from Qatar and bromine from Israel, which are critical for the lithography and manufacturing processes.

As if the management heard me, the answer came in right on point, and I was satisfied with the management’s approach to the issue in question:

In addition, given the recent situation in the Middle East, prices for certain chemicals and gases are likely to increase. Based on our current assessment, there may be impact to our profitability, but it is too early to quantify the impact.

TSMC operates a well-established enterprise risk management system to identify and assess all relevant risks and proactively implement risk mitigation strategies. In terms of material supply, TSMC's strategy is to continuously develop multi-source supply solutions to build a well-diversified global supplier base and to improve the local supply chain. For specialty chemicals and gases, including helium and hydrogen, we source from multiple suppliers in different regions, and we have prepared safety stock inventory on hand. We are also working closely with our suppliers to further strengthen the resiliency and sustainability of our supply chain. Thus, we do not expect any near-term impact on our operations for material supply.

While the management didn’t mention the bromine, which comes from Israel, I assume this two-week ceasefire (which, potentially, could become a ceasefire until the next round) will ease the shortage if it even had enough time to occur. It looks like the Taiwanese government is committed to securing an LNG supply, so the company should be fine in terms of energy as well. This is pretty much everything that could drag on a company's profitability, and now let’s move to the technology.

Advanced technologies of 7 nm and below came in 1% lower than the last quarter, and now make up 74% of the total revenues of the company. In my opinion, breakdown by the segments is even more interesting - HPC or High-Performance Computing, now is the fastest-growing platform, showing 20% growth since the last quarter, accounting for 61% of the total revenue contribution. Smartphones and the automotive segments are seeing negative growth quarter over quarter due to memory supply shortages and the market softness as a result, although the management says they see a strong demand for smartphone platforms in the next few years through 2028:

Memory price hike definitely has some impact to price sensitive end market, especially in PC and smartphone market. We did see a little bit softer market. But to share with you, all the high-end smartphones continue to do better, and this is to TSMC's advantage.

CapEx is pretty large, and is also guided to be at the high end of previous guidance for this year, as a result of the rapid growth in demand. For companies like TSMC, as I already explained earlier, there is no way to grow without investing heavily first. TSMC sells at capacity, and by the massive fab buildout, they are trying to capture this demand.

We now expect our 2026 capital budget to be towards the high end of our range of between USD 52 billion and USD 56 billion as we continue to invest heavily to support our customers' growth.

There are two ways I look at this CapEx, and two different conclusions I make from it for myself. First, speaking of the TSMC, and the cyclicality of the business, I still believe the cycle turn will come, although most likely, this cycle will be way more prolonged than we are historically used to. It doesn’t mean the company will see a negative growth or margins of 40%, as during the pandemic, but it means that all of the metrics will be way more modest than they are right now. On the other hand, we see how solid the company’s performance has been over the years, so I believe the management is right, and that the cycle is nowhere near the end.

Also, the company is in a pretty good position, money-wise, having more cash than liabilities. Inventories are growing as a result of the 2 nm technology ramp-up to meet the future demand.

For the next quarter, the management expects 10% revenue growth QoQ, which is incredible, and it would represent about a 32% year-over-year increase at the midpoint.

Valuation

Given the GAAP EPS estimate of 15.38 for this fiscal year, we get the forward P/E ratio sitting at about 23.8 times, which is in line with the sector.

On the PEG ratio, which levels the whole sector for growth, the stock is about 40% undervalued. However, it is important to be careful when looking at these metrics for cyclical companies like TSMC. As I explained in my previous article:

To understand the valuation of TSM, you have to know that when looking at companies in cyclical industries like semiconductors, the numbers always look amazing until the cycle reaches its peak. It’s not just that they’re selling more chips, but their profit margins, cash flow, and overall efficiency all increase, and the business looks perfect. As a result, the stock could look way cheaper than it actually is because the earnings are huge. If the demand starts decreasing, it would result not only in a revenue decline but also in a cash flow decrease, a margin drop, and all the metrics will follow the same chain reaction.

With the PEG ratio, if the growth slows, this valuation multiple will increase much more strongly than traditional metrics. I don’t believe it will happen any time soon, but you have to understand that this is an incredible company, at a more or less fair price.

Conclusion

I would expect the market to react way more positively to the solid earnings beat and even better guidance, but investors were unimpressed for some reason. In my opinion, it has to do with the fact that this stock has already performed incredibly this year, staying not only resilient to all of the bad news, but also managing to grow, unlike most of the market, especially in the tech/AI industries.

Given the good news, but also taking the valuation into account, I think right now we have a good entry point, which reflects the leading position TSMC holds, but also doesn’t fully price in the future, which seems to be bright in the next few years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp Q1 Earnings: Growth Without The Fireworks
data.description:
Introduction

The super-regional bank U.S. Bancorp (USB) released its first-quarter 2026 earnings yesterday amid a broad relief rally fueled by negotiations over a deal between the U.S. and Iran and the associated optimism. However, USB stock edged lower yesterday, suggesting that the results were somewhat disappointing.

U.S. Bancorp is one of the few bank stocks I own, and I have written about it more or less regularly here on Seeking Alpha. In my last article, I discussed the bank's third-quarter results, which confirmed my generally constructive attitude. All in all, I found that the valuation of USB stock largely reflected the fundamentals, which is why I downgraded it from my previous “buy” rating in mid-April 2025 to “hold.”

In this update, I will share my assessment of U.S. Bancorp's Q1 2026 growth, profitability, and, of course, credit quality—also against the backdrop of the domestic average and the continued steepening of the interest rates. Finally, I will provide a brief valuation update, highlighting why I continue to rate USB stock as “hold.”

U.S. Bancorp Q1 2026 Earnings: Steady As She Goes

First quarter earnings per share (EPS) came in at $1.18, roughly 4% ahead of consensus and up 15% year-over-year. As is mostly the case with U.S. Bancorp, there were no earnings adjustments. Net revenues were $7.3 billion for the quarter, an increase of roughly 5% year-over-year. I was encouraged to see that U.S. Bancorp once again posted healthy positive operating leverage of more than 4 percentage points.

Average total loans grew by a reassuring 2.4% compared to the prior quarter (Figure 1), whereas year-over-year, the bank delivered loan growth of 3.8%. On the earnings call, CFO John Stern expressed confidence that loan growth for the full year will likely be even stronger. Corporate banking is expected to remain the main growth driver—in this segment, USB reported year-over-year growth of 11.4%. Mortgage loans to retail customers declined by 2% compared to the previous year's first quarter, which I believe is understandable given the current environment.

Net interest income grew by 4.1% year-over-year, largely thanks to the aforementioned strong growth in commercial lending but also due to the repricing of fixed assets. The latter helped the year-over-year comparison of net interest margin, which came in at 2.77%. So even though USB's net interest margin has improved by 5 basis points, I would argue that it could be even higher. The yield curve—illustrated by daily Treasury yields in Figure 2—continues to normalize, thereby strengthening banks' ability to make an increasingly significant contribution to interest income through duration management. Of course, this applies only on the condition that credit quality is maintained, especially for long-term loans.

Regarding noninterest income, I was encouraged to see fee-based revenues up 6.9% year-over-year. I compiled an overview of the individual contributors to noninterest income in Figure 3, which confirms U.S. Bancorp's continued broad diversification and good growth in the largest domains.

Importantly, fee-based income represented more than 40% of total quarterly net revenues, and there was—once again—almost no impact from securities-related gains or losses. This confirms that a significant portion of U.S. Bancorp's revenue is not directly tied to interest rates. Given the stickiness of fee-based revenues in various segments, I would also argue that U.S. Bancorp is less sensitive to economic cycles than competitors that focus on traditional banking.

During the earnings call, management confirmed its full-year net revenue guidance in the range of 4% to 6%, indicating that fee-based revenues will also continue to grow in the mid-single-digit range.

On the liabilities side of the balance sheet, deposits remained essentially unchanged compared to the prior quarter (Figure 4) but rose by 1.7% year-over-year. Growth was primarily seen in interest-bearing deposits and across most segments. While this is certainly no cause for excitement, it is entirely appropriate and manageable given the ongoing significant loan growth on the assets side of the balance sheet.

U.S. Bancorp's efficiency ratio improved by a solid 2.6% year-over-year but rose by 80 basis points compared to the previous quarter. From a longer-term perspective (Figure 5), I believe an efficiency ratio below 60% is solid in the case at hand. However, since U.S. Bancorp has historically been among the leaner banks in the U.S., I would like to see the efficiency ratio continue to decline going forward. Given various inflationary factors, this is, of course, not easy, but the use of AI is likely to represent a net tailwind for U.S. Bancorp and the sector as a whole. At the same time, AI is certainly a longer-term risk that should be considered in connection with (partial) disruptions at banks and payment service providers.

Return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) declined by 50 basis points year-over-year and 140 basis points quarter-over-quarter but remained at a very solid level of 17.0% in the first quarter of 2026. By comparison, Truist Financial Corporation (TFC), a comparable but more traditionally oriented peer of U.S. Bancorp, recorded an ROTCE of 12.7% in 2025 (results for the first quarter of 2026 will be released today).

From the perspective of asset quality, the bank remains on solid footing, in my view. Net charge-offs were up modestly compared to the prior quarter (Figure 6) but remained unproblematic and close to the domestic average (Figure 6). Allowances for credit losses increased by $50 million, or less than 1% of the prior quarter's allowance, largely due to loan portfolio growth. Allowances in the context of commercial real estate (CRE) loans actually declined in the last two quarters, after two quarters of noticeable increases.

Looking at non-performing loans (NPL), about one-third of them are associated with commercial real estate. While this may sound problematic, the number should be viewed in the context of U.S. Bancorp's undoubtedly solid NPL ratio. Non-performing loans, expressed as a percentage of total loans and other real estate, have declined for six consecutive quarters and have declined to less than 0.4% in Q1 2026 (Figure 7).

Finally, the bank continues to maintain a solid capitalization, as evidenced by its Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio of 10.8%—unchanged from both the previous year's first quarter and the prior quarter, despite a moderate dividend hike in September and the continuation of share repurchases. In the first quarter of 2026, U.S. Bancorp repurchased approximately 5 million shares. Unlike in previous quarters, however, the repurchases were only sufficient to offset shares issued for incentive programs and other purposes, even though the volume of repurchased shares increased from $100 million to $200 million. On the earnings call, CFO Stern hinted at slightly increasing buybacks over the course of the year.

In general, U.S. Bancorp is among the banks that take a rather cautious stance toward buybacks. While a total of approximately $9 billion in dividends on common stock has been paid out over the past three years, buybacks amounted to only about $700 million.

It could be argued that U.S. Bancorp's growth, profitability, and capitalization would justify somewhat more aggressive share buybacks. However, as a long-term shareholder, I have no objection to the relatively moderate pace considering long-term financial stability. Furthermore, since U.S. Bancorp stock looks fairly valued relative to both its book value and tangible book value (Figure 8), I would argue that the return on investment for shares repurchased at current levels is not particularly convincing. Although banks are not typically valued using earnings and revenue multiples, it is probably worth noting that USB stock is also fairly valued from this perspective.

Summary And Conclusion

U.S. Bancorp posted solid results for the first quarter of 2026, driven primarily by strong loan growth, particularly in the commercial segment. Noninterest income, which is largely fee-based, rose by a healthy 7% compared to the same period last year and continues to account for more than 40% of total net revenues.

The bank once again recorded significant positive operating leverage, so Q1 2026 marks the seventh consecutive quarter of progress in the right direction. From a six-year historical perspective, USB's efficiency ratio is near the lower end of the scale. Nevertheless, I would like to see a continued focus on lean operations—after all, U.S. Bancorp has historically been among the more efficient banks in the U.S. A lean cost structure should also help the bank maintain its profitability if net interest income eventually declines along with interest rates.

Asset quality also remained solid, as did capitalization. From this perspective, the cautious stance on share buybacks seems somewhat unjustified. However, since U.S. Bancorp stock appears fairly valued based on book value and historical earnings and revenue multiples, I don't mind the focus on dividends and a strong capitalization.

Personally, I wouldn't mind if my USB holdings were slightly larger in terms of invested capital. However, given the fair valuation and the fact that there are several more attractive investment opportunities at the moment, I see no reason to upgrade USB stock to “buy.”

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Chiron Real Estate: From High-Teens To Sub-10x FFO, A Rebound Case
data.description:
Introduction

As a (contrarian) REIT investor, I solely cover REITs on Seeking Alpha. Preferably REITs that are disliked by the market and are undervalued beyond what I deem to be reasonable.

Today I want to go over one of my more recent stock purchases: Chiron Real Estate (XRN). Together with EPR Properties (EPR), Realty Income (O) and Modiv Industrial (MDV), these are now my monthly paying dividend REITs.

Today I will provide you with the core reasons why I decided to open a position in this company. One of which I already went over: the fact that XRN pays a monthly dividend. A monthly dividend provides me with more liquidity that really helps me since my wife and I don't really save a lot of money. We are still relatively young (32 and 29), so the bulk of our income is derived from good old labor. We don't have any kids (yet), and we cover our living expenses by a wide margin. A monthly dividend provides us with more flexibility to direct money where it's most needed on a month-to-month basis: a holiday, new expensive clothing, or a larger amount of money to invest into the stock market. We both have ADHD, so we really like to decide ad hoc. Since we have the financial flexibility to live our lives this way, why not?

Investing is a very personal journey, so the coming chapters will solely cover the "facts" of Chiron Real Estate. Some inside information about my personal life can provide some color on the reasons behind me and my wife's investment decision. I'm providing my fellow investors with some background information that might show you why I make certain decisions.

A lot of dividend investors are not like me. They are a (little) bit older, more risk-averse (for that reason and others), and, of course, flooded with way more life experiences than me. I'll make sure to provide all of you with a thorough analysis of this company, after which you can decide for yourself whether to invest alongside me, or skip this ticker entirely.

Let us begin!

Risk assessment

No one knows at what price a stock will be trading tomorrow or even next quarter. All we can do is analyze a company and see whether there are fundamental reasons to buy a stock (or skip on it).

Regarding REITs, the dividend (safety) and the margin of safety are important topics to decide whether to buy into a REIT or not. In order to assess these topics, we have to start with some sort of risk assessment. I'll start by going over the portfolio in order to see if we can spot some sort of overexposure in this regard.

Portfolio diversification

Chiron Real Estate focuses on investing in and managing critical healthcare properties: 72% Outpatient Medical, 16% Inpatient Rehab and 12% in "other" assets. Around 7% of the average base rent [ABR] is coming from the largest tenant (Lifepoint Health), and the top 10 tenants are responsible for 38.8% of ABR. To me this looks healthy and manageable, but if XRN could decrease its exposure to Lifepoint Health, that surely would be a good thing. 17% of ABR is derived from Texas. Since exposure to one single region is less of a deal compared to tenant exposure, I don't see any meaningful risk from a diversification perspective.

Refinancing risk

XRN has been busy 'cleaning up' the balance sheet, with no upcoming refinancing in 2026 and 2027. This provides the company with time to use retained earnings (that will grow because of rent escalators) to further deleverage the company. If rates go down in the coming years, the stock only has one way to go... up!

The stock is too cheap

REIT investors want a sustainable and, preferably, growing dividend. When you buy Chiron's stock today, you get a monthly dividend of $0.25. A 8.5% dividend yield at today's price ($35.24). Since XRN is currently focused on stabilizing its balance sheet (Feb. 2026 investor presentation, page 8), I play it safe and only expect total returns coming from the current dividend yield combined with another 2.1% coming from rent escalators (page 10). You arrive at an expected - conservative - total return of 10.6%.

REITs are leveraged vehicles, which means that they use leverage to boost earnings. When rates go up, REIT valuations go down due to opportunity costs (the 'risk free' 10-year treasury) and higher interest rates that lower [A]FFO due to higher interest expenses.

Both the tariffs and the war in Iran are pushing inflation higher, leading to lower REIT valuations because of the interest rate sensitivity (and rates go up to combat inflation). If the Democrats win the midterms, Trump's policies may become more moderate because a divided government limits his ability to push measures that some would view as being a bit aggressive. In my humble opinion, more measured policies will benefit the economy (and push inflation lower).

If things normalize, inflation declines, and rate cuts follow, I expect all REITs to get back to a more reasonable valuation, Chiron Real Estate included.

But let's dive a bit deeper, shall we.

XRN shows us that the company traded at 19.5x FFO back in Dec. 2021 compared to 8.9x in Feb. 2026. Q4 2025 FFO came in at $0.97 ($3.88 annualized). A P/FFO multiple of 9x.

But 'Funds From Operations' is a surface-level profit metric for REITs, since it does not account for no-cash adjustments such as straight-line rent, recurring cash outflows like leasing costs and maintenance capex. So it's best to compare the free cash flow using the Q4 2021 and Q4 2025 data.

Back in 2021, XRN reported adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) to provide a metric better served for dividend coverage analysis compared to FFO. The FFO number includes straight-line rent: the average rent that a tenant pays during the entire lease. Due to rent escalators, straight-line rent overstates earnings in the first half of the lease term, as reported revenue exceeds actual cash rent. When I go over the Q4 2025 numbers, the company has shifted towards 'Funds available for distribution' [FAD]. FAD goes a step further by deducting recurring capital expenditures and leasing costs (like 'tenant improvements' and 'leasing commissions') from FFO, making it an even more conservative metric (compared to AFFO) to assess the true cash available for dividend payments.

To make a (more or less) fair comparison, I decided to subtract 25% of the full-year 2021 capital expenditures on existing real estate investments as reported in the Q4 2021 supplemental presentation (page 8). This Q4 2021 number came in at $2,351 (25%), lowering the AFFO from $16,380 to $14,028. We arrive at $0.205 in FAD per share. During Q3 2025, a one-for-five reverse split was done, so I had to multiply all the Q4 2021 numbers by 5. I took the liberty of including the leverage ratio as well, because the market valuation of a REIT includes - among others - the leverage ratio. There are more metrics to look at, but I have to pick and choose. As a percentage of FFO, the dividend coverage has decreased slightly during the years, but the FAD payout ratio became healthier. The leverage crept up a little, but since we are in a higher interest-rate environment, the current leverage definitely should put pressure on the valuation of the stock. And let's not forget that during 2021, the company was making acquisitions. Today, the company is busy deleveraging the balance sheet. A REIT in 'growth-modus' is worth more, as you might have guessed.

We could reason that if rates go down, Chiron Real Estate should be a real winner since the leverage is pretty high and any windfall coming from lower interest expenses would really help the stock. The REIT could get back into growth mode, boosting future FFO and therefore the valuation of the stock.

The lower FAD payout ratio provides some cushion in the current environment, but the current dividend might not be safe enough for more risk-averse investors. On the other hand, your starting yield is very high, so you get compensated for taking on this extra risk.

It made sense for XRN to trade at richer valuations back in 2021. Rates were lower, and the company was growing. But does it make sense for this REIT to go from 19.5x all the way down to around 9x? I don't think so. Especially when you take into consideration the fact that years of inflation have pushed construction costs up by a lot (43% in the past five years). This will benefit the portfolio value of REITs in general, XRN included. It will also make it harder to get a good enough economic return that justifies building new properties, restraining supply while demand is growing (because of the aging U.S. population). This long-term tailwind benefits some of my other holdings like Healthpeak Properties (DOC) and Medical Properties Trust (MPT) as well. Investors tend to focus on the short-term, but it is important to look (years) ahead to understand where the market will probably gravitate to.

Cap rate analysis

During Q4 2025, XRN sold two non-core assets for gross proceeds of $11.3 million, resulting in a $0.4 million loss (minus 3.5%). Is this a bad thing? Well, selling at a loss might be a bad sign, but only if you compare it to the current trading range of a company's (common) equity.

A cap rate analysis gives you a sense of the equity value of a REIT portfolio. I start by taking the annualized cash net operating income (NOI) of $119 million (page 2). When I take the 2022–2025 acquisition cap rate of 7.8% and apply it to XRN, we arrive at the following company portfolio valuation: $119 million NOI / 0.078 = $1.525 billion.

If someone wants to achieve a 7.8% unleveraged return on a property, $1.5 billion is what you have to pay to acquire XRN. In order to get us to the equity value of the common shares, we need to subtract the consolidated debt ($653.9 million) and we arrive at $868 million. We also have to subtract the liquidation value of the preferred stock ($129 million at the end of 2025). We add the 'cash and cash equivalents' on the balance sheet ($9 million) and arrive at $748 million.

When dividing the $748 million across the 14.516 million shares and units outstanding, the intrinsic value comes in at $51.5 per share.

XRN has sold some non-core assets at a slight loss to book value (minus 3.5%), but equity is trading at around 32% below its intrinsic value. To me, this doesn't make a lot of sense. A clear example of Mr. Market overreacting.

Conclusion

I rate Chiron Real Estate a 'buy.' My core reasons are the following:

  • A meaningful margin of safety given the current price compared to the intrinsic value per share
  • No refinancing risk in the coming two years
  • A double-digit yield to start with (dividend + growth)
  • A monthly dividend to boost liquidity (that can be used to buy more cheap shares at a faster pace)
  • Management alignment with shareholders*

*The fact that management bought a pretty decent amount of stock themselves during 2025, is another indicator that shares are undervalued at the current price.

There are always risks when taking a position in any REIT. Regarding Chiron Real Estate, the higher leverage gives you more exposure to where interest rates are going. XRN could be viewed as a 'bet on the Fed.' If rates go up in the coming years, refinancing will become more expensive, and the dividend might be on the chopping board. But if rates go down, the reward will be bigger as well. In the meantime, the high dividend yield compensates for this risk. Whether it is enough for you, depends.

For retired dividend investors that rely on a steady dividend paycheck to pay the bills, there are better and safer REITs to pick from. But you can decide to initiate a small position, use a DCA method, or go all-in. It is really up to you. I can sleep well by making this a 5% position, but it's really personal. I will just follow management and sit on my sizable position for years to come (Ceteris Paribus).

I started covering this stock today, and I understand that there is a lot more to talk about. This certainly won't be my last article on this specific ticker. Feel free to reach out to me in the comment section below. As always, I will be there.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Salesforce: Why I Have Strong Conviction In This Stock
data.description:
Agentic AI Adoption Has Reached a Key Milestone

Even strong 4Q earnings couldn't save Salesforce, Inc. (CRM) (CRM:CA). The stock slipped to a new 52-week low this week. It's now down 29% year-to-date, wiping out half of its market cap since the 2025 high.

CRM previously suffered from a 58% peak-to-trough drawdown back in 2022, similar to the current one. If history repeats, we are close to the bottom.

This is a sentiment-driven market. Companies like Anthropic and other hyperscalers are fueling AI fears and creating volatility in the software sector. Whether or not AI can be disruptive remains to be seen, but any negative headline news can drive indiscriminate selling. I say this market mispricing creates bargains for investors who can identify long-term winners in the current agentic AI transition.

I believe CRM is well-positioned to outshine others, and its agentic AI platform has reached a major inflection. We can clearly see that the demand is increasing, with Agentforce and Data 365 contributing to higher revenue growth.

There are many other bullish signs. From the long history of achieving 40 Rule (FY2027 guidance implies 45%) to double-digit CAGR from FY26 to FY30, CRM will further expand agentic market shares over the next 5 years.

Three months ago, I upgraded the stock to Strong Buy after 3Q earnings. I'm surprised to see Agentforce adoptions were up nearly 50% sequentially, with 330% YoY ARR growth. Although large-scale AI monetization takes time to materialize, I saw an early revenue inflection at that time.

Some critics argue that software stocks should structurally trade at cheap valuations after the recent AI boom. At the sector level, it could happen, but it's not necessary to apply to every individual software stock. CRM's 14x non-GAAP P/E TTM is well below the S&P 500 utility and consumer staples sectors. I know it's not a direct comparison, but its P/E is even cheaper than the Chinese internet sector. Therefore, I think the stock is due for a rebound, and my Strong Buy still holds.

Agentforce 360 Ecosystem Is a Major Growth Driver

As I highlighted above, even with a sequential slowdown in Slack revenue in 4Q FY2026, "Agentforce 360 platform, Slack, and Other" revenue jumped 37% YoY to $2.7 billion on a constant currency basis. The segment is growing rapidly and now represents one fourth of CRM's revenue.

Agentforce and Data 360 ARR (including Informatica) reached $2.9 billion in 4Q, up 200% YoY. We saw 60% of Agentforce and Data 360 bookings came from contract expansion from existing customers.

Based on the current trend, I expect CRM's revenue growth will at least not decelerate in the next two years. In fact, the guided FY2027 revenue outlook implies a second consecutive year of acceleration.

Data Edge Can Make a Difference

Investors dumped CRM simply because of fears that third-party AI-generated code and agentic AI services will kill traditional software companies and permanently lower the sector's growth outlook.

In my recent article, I highlighted companies that can differentiate through their own data moats and adapt quickly to agentic AI will eventually survive.

So far, I don't think Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude will pose a major threat to CRM's Data 360, which is the foundational data layer for Agentforce. Regardless of which data repositories you are using, you can integrate them into Data 360 to harmonize your data. This creates higher switching costs for existing customers.

Moreover, CRM pivoted to agentic AI back in 2024, which gave them an "early mover" advantage in retaining customers and making a smooth transition to its own agentic AI platform.

Bookings and Backlogs Indicate Higher Demand for Agentic AI

CRM is an ultimate beneficiary of the agentic AI era, because the company's net new annual order value growth has been accelerating since FY2024 (the year of pivoting to agentic AI) and reached a positive 10% YoY growth in FY2026, driven by higher contract values and lower attrition rates.

The underlying demand can also be reflected by bookings and remaining performance obligations. Remaining performance obligations, or RPO, are the total amount of backlogs that will be recognized as future revenue. Current RPO (cRPO) is the value of the contract that will be realized within the next 12 months. For CRM, cRPO represents nearly 50% of its total RPO in 4Q FY2026.

We can see cRPO accelerated to 16.2% YoY in 4Q. Although they didn't provide booking numbers, we can calculate it by adding the sequential change in RPO balance to total revenue, which implies 23% YoY of current bookings growth in 4Q.

Based on 1Q FY2027 guidance, cRPO is expected to grow 14% YoY. However, this only implies a 5% YoY current bookings growth. However, this doesn't look too bad since the slowdown is driven by a high basis of cRPO balance from the previous quarter.

I believe the robust current bookings and cRPO will signal a strong revenue outlook in FY2027.

FY2027 Still Looks Very Solid

The upward trend in demand backlogs is expected to lift FY2027 revenue growth to 10.5% YoY, slightly up from 10% YoY in FY2026. The non-GAAP EPS would slow sharply to 5% next year due to a flat margin outlook. I think this is because the company will continue to invest heavily in agentic AI.

Its FCF would grow 9.5% YoY in FY2027, which translates to a 34.2% FCF margin, similar to 34.7% in FY2026. However, we should know that CRM's most investments are from M&A rather than capital expenditure, which can inflate free cash flow.

This is a High-Quality Company with a Cheap Valuation

CRM's 13x non-GAAP P/E is 43% below the sector median, showing a distressed valuation. Since the Seeking Alpha Charting page doesn't have non-GAAP multiples, I'll use the "cash flow from operations" multiple as a reference.

Its P/CF TTM is at 10.5x, down 60% from its 5-year average and currently at an all-time low. Compared to software leader Microsoft (MSFT), CRM is trading at a 40% discount.

To boost shareholder value, the company issued a $50 billion stock buyback and raised its quarterly dividend by 5.8% YoY in 4Q FY2026. This strongly demonstrates the management's confidence in the stock's long-term value.

The Bottom Line

With that being said, I strongly believe that CRM's backlog growth, cash flow quality, and distressed valuation together deserve a Strong Buy. Despite SaaSpocalypse fears, the stock is extremely undervalued given its early success of agentic AI transition and robust demand for Agentforce and Data 360. The P/E trading deeply below utility and staple sectors doesn't make sense to me. We've already seen the software sector start to bounce back from the recent lows. As sentiment improves, this is now a good time to be more aggressive on CRM.

data.title: Risk Event, Not Regime Shift: Why Markets Are Showing Resilience
data.description:
Iran tensions have pushed both oil prices and market volatility higher, but stocks have clawed back every point lost during the conflict. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist, TD Wealth maps three scenarios for energy prices and breaks down what the rapid sentiment rebound is really signaling.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - Equities have recovered the ground they lost due to the Middle East conflict. But should investors be expecting a choppy ride ahead, or can markets expect to show us some more resiliency? There's a lot going on there. Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist, TD Wealth, always great to have you in the studio, my friend.

Brad Simpson - Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - I was just sitting -- and from a layman's perspective, I was just watching the action of the past month. There's this trigger event. It starts in Washington in terms of some sort of policy. And it just reminded me of last year. There was a trigger event. And what happened a month later in April - policy, the market sold off, and the markets regained almost in short order. How should we be reading this market?

Brad Simpson - Actually, no, I think that's a really great tie-in, actually, between the two. If you look at Liberation Day, maybe one of the most astonishing things was that the surprise of what was being implemented and then the correction occurred. It's kind of like it was telegraphed.

And then you fast-forward to here, and you've got two aircraft carriers sitting outside of Iran. The Polymarkets had it at 68% that by the end of March, there was going to be some sort of either escalation of some sort into Iran by the United States and Israel. So I think the starting point of that was, unlike Liberation Day, I think we really looked at the market reaction to it. I would say the market has been remarkably resilient.

Forget that. Let's go to the lows of this market and say, you're -- if you wanted to find the perfect day of the trade from top to bottom on the S&P 500, you can go from the highest, highest ever to the low. It would be a 10% correction. You'd probably, if we're being serious and go -- it's around our 6% or 7% correction for that.

And I think -- and a little bit of when we look at that, I think that the difference is that-- number one is that as we were moving through the fall of 2025, and you and I had talked about this, and we've written about at length, and we put our money where our mouth was on this -- was saying that the US economy, the Canadian economy, the European economy, and then even going into emerging markets, were starting to improve and starting to get -- and the more you looked at it, you started to go that it's been -- particularly in the United States, you got this early- to mid-stage economy turn-up. And the market in the last fall started to broaden out and started to move away from those big tech names. We're talking ultimately, about the software correction.

And so what was happening is you've had a market correction where really, what was happening is money was moving out of the growth and momentum. And what it was moving in towards was the broader market and moving into -- and that move, I think, almost accounts for more of the correction that we saw. And it was just a readjustment. And between the market expecting it and what was going on inside of that market, I think that -- you can explain away a lot of that here. And then you got to go, okay, now that we've done that, where do we go from here?

Greg Bonnell - Let's talk about where we go from here because the news flow is so intense on a daily basis. When I can read a headline that says the market regains its losses of the conflict, and for a day, there's not a crazy headline that makes me worry about everything, I take a sigh of relief. And I want to pretend that everything's going to be great. But let's talk about it. Is everything going to be great?

Brad Simpson - No, I don't think everything's going to be great. And I thought we would answer this. And you know that I don't have the power of foresight. But I thought I would bring this along. And what we're looking at is a scenario analysis that will be coming out in our quarterly strategy at the end of the quarter. So -- again, about a week or so here.

But looking at this, the most important one I want us to look at is description. So let's call that time. And key driver, let's call that inflation, sticky inflation or stagflation. These are the major determinants. So this is looking at going, what's going to happen in -- if we have this blockade that leads to a quick resolution, that's the bull case. And I'd be a little -- and in both cases, put a -- let's call it 20% scenario here, rapid de-escalation. Oil starts panning out to $70 or $80 a barrel. This is going from the end -- the beginning of this year, January 31 to December 31, 2026.

You would see the S&P 500, a bull case, could be up 24%. For the year, TSX up 19%. Look at the base case then, call it 90 to 110. You look at S&P 16 -- up 11%. What you're looking at there is you have stickier inflation. So interest rates-- instead of declining, which you do in the bull case, you see them staying where they're at.

The bear case is that it -- this gets longer and prolonged. And this gets $120 barrel of oil. So growth starts to -- not only does growth slow, you start to look at not inflation, but you start to see stagflation. So you see slower growth and higher inflation. What you see there is you actually see interest rates move up because you're kind of in this shock. And then you start to see them go down after that. And that's where you see a correction of call it the S&P 500, 15% and 19% there.

At least it starts to frame out what are the things to really look for. Duration, right? Time. How long does this play out because as long as it plays out, it impacts the oil price. And as long as the oil price -- it impacts on -- right now, inflation isn't a problem. That $90 to $100, inflation gets a little stickier. And that base case is where we're at right now.

And so I am comfortable with saying that is -- but if this gets -- if this becomes something more longer term, which it has a real potential to. I'm not sticking my head in the sand on this one. But at least, I think I wanted for your viewers to kind of go let's frame it out in nowhere. And then we know how we're going to build -- make decisions. And I think that's a terrific start.

Greg Bonnell - Since we don't know exactly where we end up in that frame, is there a sense there that sentiment is getting tested and that, perhaps, people are getting a little cautious in the markets?

Brad Simpson - You would think. But the reality is, we see something quite different than that. And I brought something -- I brought a -- I don't know if you guys have it. But I brought a chart along that we can actually look at there. So let's look at this.
